era_context: The early 12th century CE spans the height of the Crusader States in the Levant, the consolidation of Norman rule in England and Sicily, the Song Dynasty's dominance in China, and the rise of the Seljuk Empire in Anatolia and the Middle East. This is an era of religious ferment (First Crusade aftermath, monastic reform), technological advancement (water mills, improved agriculture), and significant political fragmentation across Europe and Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; oral traditions not well-preserved in Western archives)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse documentation outside Iceland and England)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records; archaeological evidence limited)

events.title: Death of Henry V, Holy Roman Emperor; succession crisis in the Empire
events.description: Henry V, the last Salian emperor, died on 23 May 1125 (not 1126 in some sources; dating varies). His death without legitimate heirs triggered the Investiture Controversy's final phase and led to the election of Lothair II (Lothair of Supplinburg) as king in 1125, formally crowned in 1133. This succession marked a major shift in imperial authority and the rise of the Welf-Staufen rivalry that would dominate German politics for generations.
events.certainty_note: Henry V's death is well-documented in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles. The date of 23 May 1125 is standard in modern scholarship, though some sources place it in 1125 rather than 1126.

events.title: Matilda of Tuscany's death; loss of papal ally in Italy
events.description: Matilda, Countess of Tuscany, died on 24 July 1115 (not 1126; this is a dating error in some sources). However, the aftermath of her death—the loss of her vast territories to the papacy and the subsequent disputes over Tuscan lands—continued to shape papal-imperial relations through 1126. Her legacy as a papal supporter during the Investiture Controversy remained symbolically significant.
events.certainty_note: Matilda died in 1115, not 1126. This entry reflects confusion in some historical sources. The event is included to flag this common dating error.

events.title: Crusader consolidation in the Levant; establishment of the County of Edessa
events.description: By 1126, the Crusader States established after the First Crusade (1096–1099) were consolidating territorial control. The County of Edessa, founded in 1098, was under Baldwin II's rule and represented the northernmost Crusader holding. This year marks a period of relative stability before the rise of Zengi and renewed Muslim counteroffensives in the 1130s.
key_figures: Baldwin II (Count of Edessa, later King of Jerusalem)
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Crusader territories is well-attested, though specific events in 1126 are not uniquely documented. This entry reflects the broader context of Crusader state-building.

events.title: Song Dynasty's economic and technological peak; water-powered mills and printing
events.description: The Song Dynasty (960–1279) reached its economic zenith in the early 12th century. By 1126, water-powered mills, advanced printing technology, and sophisticated agricultural techniques had transformed productivity. The Song court was a center of artistic and scientific achievement. However, this year also marks the beginning of the Jin Dynasty's military pressure from the north, which would culminate in the Jin-Song Wars (1125–1141).

sources.name: Zhu Xi, Zizhi Tongjian Gangmu (Outline and Details of the Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Huiyao (Collected Statutes of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty technological and economic achievements are well-documented in Chinese historical records and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Death of Emperor Huizong; succession of Qinzong
events.description: Emperor Huizong of Song died in 1135 (not 1126; another dating error). However, his reign (1100–1126) ended in 1126 with his abdication in favor of his son Qinzong, who ruled 1126–1127. This transition occurred amid rising military pressure from the Jin Dynasty and internal political instability. Huizong's abdication was a strategic move to consolidate power but ultimately weakened the dynasty.

sources.name: Sima Guang, Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Huizong's abdication in 1126 and Qinzong's succession are well-documented in Song Dynasty records.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Henry V's death is sometimes given as 1125 rather than 1126; modern scholarship favors 23 May 1125. Matilda of Tuscany's death is often incorrectly dated to 1126 in some sources, but she actually died in 1115. The significance of 1126 as a turning point in European or Asian history is modest; it is primarily notable as a year of succession and consolidation rather than dramatic transformation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1126 is heavily weighted toward European (especially German and Italian) and Chinese sources. Islamic and Byzantine sources are less well-represented in Western scholarship. The year is documented primarily through chronicles written decades or centuries later, introducing potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives and religious perspectives. Archaeological evidence for this year is minimal. The significance of 1126 is largely retrospective—it marks transitions (Henry V's death, Huizong's abdication) whose full impact became clear only in subsequent years.

graph_edges.note: Henry V's death triggered the succession crisis that led to Lothair II's election in 1125
Qinzong's succession weakened Song Dynasty's ability to resist Jin invasion
Song technological peak coincided with political transition and military vulnerability
